race.name: L'Etape du Tour
race.tagline: Ride a Tour de France mountain stage on fully closed roads — the closest an amateur gets to the real thing.

vitals.location: Bourg d'Oisans → Alpe d'Huez (via Col de Sarenne)
vitals.registration: Open registration, first-come-first-served. Sells out quickly.
vitals.entry_fee: EUR 159 base (EUR 231 with jersey and medal)
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple stations before/after major climbs — energy gels, bars, fruit, water, Coca-Cola, Maurten. Pasta party at finish.
vitals.cutoff_time: ~12-13 hours from first start. Intermediate cutoffs at key climbs. Broom wagon sweeps.

climate.description: Valley floor 20-30°C, mid-altitude 15-22°C, Galibier summit 5-12°C even in July. 20°C+ temperature swing on race day. 35% rain chance. Afternoon thunderstorms are a significant risk.
challenges: 25-30°C heat in valleys, near-freezing at Galibier 2,642m — massive temperature range
Long, fast descents cause severe wind chill — mandatory jacket enforcement
Afternoon thunderstorm risk builds over alpine ridges

terrain.surface: Excellent French national and departmental roads. Exception: Col de Sarenne section narrower with rougher patches.
features: 4 major cols plus Alpe d'Huez finish via Col de Sarenne
Col du Galibier at 2,642m — one of cycling's most famous climbs
Col de la Croix de Fer HC — 27.6km approach
Fully closed to all motorized traffic
Course changes every year — replicates a different TdF mountain stage

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Prestige 5 confirmed (prestige_5_events list). ASO organization is military-grade: fully closed roads, broom wagon, Tour de France production values. Road surface scored 1 (excellent tarmac = low difficulty). Cultural impact 5 — THE Tour de France amateur experience, 30+ editions, global L'Etape series in 25 countries. 2026 is one of the toughest editions ever with Galibier + Alpe d'Huez.

key_climbs.name: Col de la Croix de Fer
key_climbs.tactical_notes: First major block. Three distinct sections. Iron cross monument at summit. Featured in TdF 19+ times since 1947.

key_climbs.name: Col du Télégraphe
key_climbs.tactical_notes: Steady, consistent gradient through wooded switchbacks. Gateway to the Galibier. Short descent to Valloire before Galibier begins.

key_climbs.name: Col du Galibier
key_climbs.tactical_notes: Highest point on course. One of cycling's most famous climbs. Henri Desgrange memorial. TdF since 1911. Altitude effects significant above 2,200m. Cold even in July.

key_climbs.name: Col de Sarenne
key_climbs.tactical_notes: Narrow road, no guardrails in places. Controversial when used in 2013 TdF. Rarely used — makes this edition truly special.

key_climbs.name: Alpe d'Huez (finish)
key_climbs.tactical_notes: Riders arrive via the 'back door' through Sarenne, not the traditional 21 bends. Short final ramp to finish. Riders descend the famous 21 bends as a cooldown.

course_description.character: A Tour de France mountain stage on fully closed roads — the closest an amateur cyclist can get to the real thing. The 2026 edition is one of the most demanding in L'Etape history: four major cols culminating in the Galibier at 2,642m, then the rarely-used Col de Sarenne before arriving at Alpe d'Huez. The course changes every year, keeping the experience fresh.
course_description.signature_challenge: Col du Galibier — 17.5km at 7% average to 2,642m. After already climbing the Croix de Fer and Télégraphe, this is where altitude meets accumulated fatigue. Near-freezing conditions possible at the summit even in July.
suffering_zones: Col du Galibier km 12-17.5: altitude effects above 2,200m compound the 7% gradient
Col de Sarenne: 8.7% average with 16% max on a narrow road after 145km
Col de la Croix de Fer km 20-27: the final push to 2,067m comes after the hardest section at Maupas (11%)

logistics.airport: Grenoble-Isère (GNB, 1hr 10min, limited flights), Lyon Saint-Exupéry (LYS, 2hr 20min, major hub), Geneva (GVA, 2hr 40min, major hub)
logistics.lodging_strategy: Bourg d'Oisans (start): EUR 80-150/night hotels, limited supply. Alpe d'Huez (finish): EUR 120-250/night ski resort accommodation. Book 6-12 months ahead. Tour operator packages EUR 299-4,399+.
logistics.transport: Event shuttles between start and finish. Baggage transport. Parking at Bourg d'Oisans. After finishing, riders descend Alpe d'Huez 21 bends to return.
logistics.food: Pasta party at finish included in entry. Feed zones with Maurten nutrition, fruit, water, Coca-Cola.

history.origin_story: Conceived by ASO (Amaury Sport Organisation), the same company behind the Tour de France. Vision: allow amateur cyclists to ride a complete TdF mountain stage on fully closed roads. Inaugural edition July 11, 1993: Tarbes to Pau, 208km over the Col du Tourmalet, 1,700 participants.
notable_moments: 1993: Inaugural edition — 1,700 riders over Col du Tourmalet
2000: Mont Ventoux edition in extreme heat — iconic
2003: 10th anniversary at Alpe d'Huez, record 8,500 participants
2006: Dual-event format introduced to meet demand
2025: L'Etape also held alongside Tour de France Femmes
Greg LeMond rode 2007 edition; Damon Hill (F1 champion) competed 2023-2024
history.reputation: Often called 'the world's greatest sportive' and 'the most famous mass participation bike ride in the world.' The closest an amateur can get to the Tour de France experience. 30+ L'Etape Series events worldwide in 25 countries feed riders toward the flagship French event.

biased_opinion.verdict: The Tour de France for the rest of us — and the 2026 edition might be the best yet.
biased_opinion.summary: L'Etape du Tour is the gold standard for sportive prestige. You're riding the same roads as the Tour, fully closed to traffic, with ASO's military-grade production behind you. The course changes every year, which means each edition is unique. The 2026 route — Galibier + Sarenne + Alpe d'Huez — is genuinely savage. EUR 159 for a day on the Tour is remarkable value.
strengths: Fully closed roads — the only gran fondo backed by Tour de France production
Course changes annually — each edition is a different TdF mountain stage
ASO organization is best-in-class: broom wagon, timing, feed zones, Road Captains
Galibier at 2,642m with Alpe d'Huez finish — bucket-list climbs in one day
EUR 159 base entry is extraordinary value for what you get
Global L'Etape Series in 25 countries feeds the community
weaknesses: Sells out quickly — registration timing is critical
Accommodation in Oisans valley sells out months in advance
Broom wagon is real — get pulled if you miss intermediate cutoffs
5,400m of climbing in the 2026 edition is genuinely brutal for recreational riders
biased_opinion.bottom_line: L'Etape is the most accessible Tier 1 experience in the gran fondo world. The TdF brand, ASO production, and fully closed roads create something no other sportive can replicate. If you can climb and you can get a spot, do it.

final_verdict.one_liner: Ride a Tour de France mountain stage on fully closed roads — L'Etape is the closest an amateur gets to the real thing.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes, if you can handle 5,400m of climbing. The 2026 edition is one of the toughest ever — Galibier + Sarenne + Alpe d'Huez requires serious fitness. Intermediate cutoffs mean you can't just survive; you need to maintain pace. But even mid-pack riders finish within the time limit in most years.
final_verdict.alternatives: La Marmotte (same region, similar climbing, longer tradition), Maratona dles Dolomites (lottery entry, 7 Dolomite passes), Haute Route Alps (7-day version for the truly committed).

citations.label: Epic Road Rides — tips, logistics, history
